# Questions in Livestream 79

Live Stream #79: Another Vague Cleopatra Tomb Sighting

Time links open the YouTube video at the relevant timestamp. The links use HTML anchors so GitHub can render them with `target="_blank"`.

| Time | Question | Short answer / answer direction | Expanded answer |
|---:|---|---|---|
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=371" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">6:11</a> | Why does Balaam not seem to react more strongly when his donkey talks in Numbers 22? | He said the text never says Balaam did not react, and a prophet already accustomed to strange divine encounters may not find one more miracle shocking. | He cautioned that the passage never explicitly says Balaam was calm. Still, Balaam was a prophet who had already dealt with divine communication and spiritual powers, so a talking donkey would not necessarily be uniquely shocking in his world. Falk also noted that the donkey sees the angel, is given speech to warn Balaam, and that the scene is more than a simple fable, though he did not attempt a full interpretation without more time in the text. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=726" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12:06</a> | Which parts of his Ark of the Covenant book does he most recommend? | He especially recommended chapter 6, with chapter 1 as a strong short introduction. | His favorite recommendation was chapter 6, "The Ark and other extraterrestrial vehicles," because he said that is where the whole book comes together and includes some of its best photographs. He also recommended the opening chapter, especially the short section on Bezalel, because it gives a compact story-shaped introduction to ancient Egypt in the Israelites' setting. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=997" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">16:37</a> | Is Deuteronomy 4:24 an example of Zoroastrian influence? | He said the chronology is wrong for Zoroastrian influence on Deuteronomy, though later books such as Daniel or Ezra-Nehemiah could raise different questions. | Falk said the main problem is chronological: Zoroastrianism emerges as an important religion too late to explain Deuteronomy, especially if Deuteronomy is not dated extremely late. He allowed that questions about Zoroastrian influence might be more plausible for later biblical contexts such as Daniel or Ezra-Nehemiah, but he saw no good case for it in Deuteronomy 4:24. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=1199" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">19:59</a> | What is life? | He reframed the question theologically: life is connection to God, Christ, the Spirit, other people, and creation, while separation from God is death. | He distinguished biological life from experiential and eternal life, then answered with Ecclesiastes, the Gospels, and Revelation in view. Life, in his answer, is knowing and enjoying God, receiving the life Christ gives, and being connected to God, the Spirit, community, one another, and creation; death is separation from God. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=1459" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">24:19</a> | Does Paul's use of Plato mean the Bible accepts Platonism or Plato's philosophical and political ideas? | No. He said biblical authors can quote or engage correct ideas from outside sources without endorsing the whole source. | He framed Paul's use of Greek material as intertextual engagement with the Hellenistic world, not approval of everything Plato wrote. Plato could get some things right through logic and general revelation, but quoting or using a correct idea from Plato, First Enoch, or the Hymn to the Aten does not make the whole source inspired or give blanket sanction to its wider philosophy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=1767" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">29:27</a> | Why do Orthodox Jews call Jesus "Yeshu" rather than "Yeshua," and which name best represents how he would have been addressed in his time? | He treated "Yeshu" and "Yeshua" as abbreviated forms and said Jesus would have been called something like Yahushua in the first century. | Falk first questioned whether the asker meant Orthodox Jews or Messianic Jews, then treated the names as abbreviated forms related to Joshua. He said Yeshua comes from Yahushua, that Yeshu is also an abbreviation, and that in the first century Jesus would have been addressed by a form like Yahushua, later moving through Greek and Latin forms into "Jesus." |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=2012" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">33:32</a> | Did Nebuchadnezzar set up a canopy at Tahpanhes as in Jeremiah 43:9-10, and did he take spoil from Egypt or Elephantine as in Ezekiel 29:19? | He said the canopy may refer to a war tent or base of operations, and he is more confident that Babylonian forces took spoil from Egypt. | Falk said the evidence for Nebuchadnezzar's army raiding Egypt is thin but present, and he distinguished Nebuchadnezzar personally going to Egypt from Babylonian forces operating there. He explained Tahpanhes as a northeastern Delta site where a war tent, pavilion, or base of operations could fit Jeremiah's imagery, and he was more confident that Egypt was plundered, citing reduced temple bark splendor after Assyrian, Babylonian, and Persian pressures as part of the pattern. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=2547" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">42:27</a> | What does he think of UsefulCharts saying Moses existed but the Exodus was a tale and there was no conquest? | He said there is evidence relevant to conquest claims, including Hazor, Jericho, Mount Ebal, and early Israelite settlement, and called the view odd. | He did not review the specific video in detail, but said a view that keeps Moses while rejecting both Exodus and conquest is strange. As evidence relevant to conquest, he listed the Late Bronze destruction at Hazor, Jericho evidence and site leveling, the Mount Ebal site, and the sudden appearance of early Israelite settlements in the late thirteenth and early twelfth centuries. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=2745" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">45:45</a> | What does he think about the growing rift between biblical ancient Near Eastern studies and theology departments? | He said the split is real and is often worsened by institutional finances and the dominance of New Testament priorities in seminaries. | Falk said ancient Near Eastern specialists once had a clearer home in theology departments, but financial pressure and professional self-protection have often pushed schools toward New Testament-heavy staffing and away from ancient context. He argued theologians need ancient Near Eastern studies because the Bible's historical and cultural context is often not spelled out in the text, and without that context modern readers can impose their own assumptions on ancient literature. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=3464" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">57:44</a> | Is the Canaanite comb found at Tel Lachish as revolutionary as its finders say? | He said it is interesting evidence for writing and Egypt-Canaan trade around 1700 BC, but he would wait for peer-reviewed publication before calling it revolutionary. | After clarifying that the question was about the comb, not a tomb, Falk identified it as an ivory lice comb from Lachish, roughly 1700 BC, with a Canaanite alphabetic sentence. He said it matters because it shows writing in Canaan and trade connections with Egypt around Dynasty 13, but he withheld stronger claims until a peer-reviewed publication, especially for the epigraphy. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=4095" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:08:15</a> | When does he date the life of David, and does he know good scholars on the Davidic and First Temple periods? | He gave a rough reign date beginning around 1008 BC, but the transcript renders the endpoint unclearly as "9.96"; he would need to look up current specialist recommendations. | Falk reframed the first part as the reign of King David and gave a tentative range starting around 1008 BC, with the endpoint unclear in the transcript as "9.96." He stressed that Davidic dating is rough because the biblical forty-year reign number may function idiomatically and could represent something closer to thirty-eight years. He did not name specialists from memory, saying he would need to look them up because he had not recently done deep work on the Davidic and First Temple period. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=4276" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:11:16</a> | Was Israel unusual among ancient cultures for recording many of its own failures? | Yes. He said Israel was exceptionally unusual because its religious institutions could critique the civil monarchy in a way other Near Eastern royal systems did not. | He contrasted Israel with Assyrian, Hittite, and Egyptian royal habits: some cultures might note predecessors' failures, but kings did not normally record their own, and Egyptians avoided failure language in temple contexts. Israel was unusual because its theocratic institutions, priests, and prophets could record the sins and failures of kings as part of Yahweh's governance rather than as royal self-praise. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=4671" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:17:51</a> | Would Mosaic authorship be unlikely because the Torah reflects multilingual law, history, finance, culture, and literary skill despite low literacy and limited document access? | No. He argued Moses' elite Egyptian setting would have given him scribal training and access to administrative, legal, literary, and multilingual materials. | Falk answered that elite literacy is the key exception to general low literacy. In his reconstruction, Moses grew up in an elite administrative setting connected to the vizier's household at Avaris, with access to scribal education, Egyptian language training, local Semitic dialects, probably Akkadian, and reader copies or training texts, making the Torah's range of skills plausible for him. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=5186" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:26:26</a> | Why do the apostles often preach Christ from less obvious Old Testament texts rather than clear messianic prophecies such as Isaiah 53? | He suggested they chose texts tailored to their arguments and that using more obscure passages reflects careful, literate, methodical writing rather than ignorance of obvious texts. | Falk suggested the apostles often used texts that fit the specific argument they were making rather than relying only on the obvious passages everyone already knew. He took their use of less obvious texts as evidence of deep scriptural literacy, careful reasoning, and high christology, comparing their style to the didactic and textually detailed work of early church fathers. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=5714" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:35:14</a> | Did the Magi use astrology to find Yeshua, is astrology prohibited in the Bible, and why were the Magi not condemned? | He said they almost certainly used a codified ancient form of astrology; it was not condoned for Israel, but the Magi were pagans whom God used without first correcting every practice. | Falk said the Magi almost certainly used ancient astrology, but not in the free-form modern sense; he described it as a codified system of conjunctions and interpretation books. He said Israel was not to adopt Canaanite religious practices such as astrology, but the Magi were pagan outsiders, and God could use them without first stopping to condemn every mistaken practice they had. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=6352" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:45:52</a> | Who from church history has too little written about them and potential for more research? | He mentioned Chrysostom, Ambrose, Japanese martyrs, and Julius Africanus as possible subjects, depending on source availability and prior coverage. | Falk said a common research path is to find a lesser-known figure and expand the available work, but source availability matters. He thought Chrysostom and Ambrose could use strong biographies despite existing sources, mentioned Japanese martyrs as needing more contextual English treatment, and also raised Julius Africanus as an interesting figure for further work. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=6682" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">1:51:22</a> | Does Numbers 33:3 mentioning Ramesses and Numbers 13:22 mentioning Zoan prove scribes did not redact toponyms, and is Zoan the best anachronism? | He said Zoan is likely an editorial gloss, while Ramesses being left intact suggests scribes added explanatory notes more than they redacted the underlying text. | Falk identified Zoan with Tanis and said it did not exist before Dynasty 21, making Numbers 13:22 likely an explanatory editorial gloss. His point was that scribes seem to have resisted fully updating or redacting the older text: Ramesses remains in Numbers 33, while a later note about Zoan helps readers who no longer knew the older geography. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=7221" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:00:21</a> | Is Cameron Bertuzzi's conversion to Catholicism part of a broader trend among intellectual Protestants, and has Thomism caused controversy in seminaries? | He said the trend is happening more, partly because of Protestant failures, but he was more concerned with Bertuzzi's stated reasons than the conversion itself. | Falk said some Protestant intellectual movement toward Catholicism is real, and that Thomism or similar philosophical systems can cross denominational lines. His main concern was not conversion in itself, since he allowed that people may have legitimate personal or spiritual reasons, but Bertuzzi's stated reasons, especially what Falk saw as poor exegesis and a desire to put the Old Testament behind the New. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=8106" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:15:06</a> | What about wax tablets? | He described wax tablets as reusable ancient note-taking devices, often used for temporary notes that could later be copied onto papyrus for permanent storage. | He compared wax tablets to an ancient reusable notebook: a wooden frame filled with wax that could be written on with a stylus, covered, preserved for a time, and then erased by warming or smoothing the wax. Falk said scribes may have carried both papyrus and wax tablets, using wax for daily notes and transferring important material to papyrus later. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=8535" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:22:15</a> | Is eschatology a waste of time? | He said studying eschatology is not a waste, but an excessive focus that displaces the rest of Scripture is spiritually unhealthy. | Falk answered "yes and no": eschatology can be profitable and Revelation itself promises blessing to its readers, but obsessive focus on end-times questions can make secondary issues primary. He criticized approaches that ignore the rest of Scripture, use poor exegesis, or treat eschatology as the whole Christian life rather than one part of biblical study. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=8779" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:26:19</a> | Is the Egyptian myth about Ra creating the sun and moon and then slapping the moon with a rabbit genuine? | He said the eyes of Ra element reflects a late Egyptian myth, but the rabbit element appears to be a later or cross-cultural mixture, not Egyptian. | Falk said the question was only half true. He knew of a late Egyptian myth in which Ra's eyes are connected with the sun and moon, but he had not seen an Egyptian association of a rabbit with the moon, instead noting rabbit-on-the-moon traditions elsewhere such as Japan. He suggested the rabbit detail may come from later syncretism or retelling rather than Egyptian myth. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=9220" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:33:40</a> | Would he consider doing a series or shorts about spiritual junk food? | Yes. He said a video or follow-up series on spiritual junk food could fit his earlier critique of shallow apologetics and church teaching. | Falk said he would consider at least a single video, and maybe a follow-up series, because the idea connects with his earlier "seven deadly sins of the YouTube apologist" critique. He argued that spiritual junk food affects churches, pulpits, lay growth, and neighbors, so the topic could have practical relevance beyond online apologetics. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=10370" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:52:50</a> | What was Bach's favorite instrument, or which instrument did he start with? | He did not know and said he is not a music historian; he mainly plays Bach from sheet music on guitar. | Falk said he did not know Bach's favorite instrument or starting instrument and did not claim expertise in music history. His own connection to Bach is practical rather than historical: he takes collections of Bach sheet music and plays them on guitar, while only guessing that Bach may have composed on piano or harpsichord. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=10477" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:54:37</a> | Did Egyptian religion treat its gods as gender-fluid, and does the Bible reference that idea? | No. He said the Bible does not reference it, and Egyptian deities had fixed genders even when some iconography used attributes associated with the opposite sex. | Falk said the Bible does not reference the idea and called the gender-fluidity claim a misunderstanding of Egyptian iconography. A god could display attributes associated with fertility or another gender, such as Hapi having breasts, but the texts still identify that deity consistently as male or female; he saw no convincing evidence for gender fluidity in official Egyptian religion, only some modern interpretations that lean too heavily on iconography without the texts. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=10734" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">2:58:54</a> | Is Esther a pagan name? | No. He said it is not related to Ishtar, but is a Semitic name connected with myrtle and is not theophoric. | Falk rejected the claim that Esther is derived from Ishtar. He described it as a Semitic name connected with myrtle, not as a pagan or theophoric name, and compared it with other Hebrew or Semitic names based on plants or qualities rather than deities. |
| <a href="https://youtu.be/xPyCgeAk_F8?t=10940" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">3:02:20</a> | What technical field is most useful for someone who wants to work in history, archaeology, or languages? | He recommended programming first, with database work as another especially useful technical skill for archaeology and digital humanities. | Falk recommended programming because it lets historians, archaeologists, linguists, and digital humanities workers make technology serve their research. He also emphasized database administration for digs, where someone must design schemas, run input systems, and aggregate field data, and he mentioned skills such as Python, JavaScript, SQL, system administration, data analytics, and GIS as useful technical tools. |
